problem.title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
problem.description: Given the root of a binary search tree, and an integer k, return the kth smallest value (1-indexed) of all the values of the nodes in the tree.

example.explanation: In-order traversal of BST gives sorted order: 1,2,3,4. 1st smallest is 1.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Inorder Traversal [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Perform an inorder traversal of the BST, which visits nodes in ascending sorted order by the BST property. A counter incremented on each visit identifies when the kth node is reached, at which point its value is recorded and further recursion is unnecessary. The key insight is that inorder traversal of a BST is equivalent to iterating over its elements in sorted order, so the kth visit is exactly the kth smallest element.
solutions.code_python:
# class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0): self.val=val; self.left=self.right=None
from typing import Optional

def kth_smallest(root: Optional['TreeNode'], k: int) -> int:
    count = 0
    result = 0

    def inorder(node: Optional['TreeNode']) -> None:
        nonlocal count, result
        if node is None:
            return
        inorder(node.left)
        count += 1
        if count == k:
            result = node.val
            return
        inorder(node.right)

    inorder(root)
    return result

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Iterative Inorder with Stack [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Use an explicit stack to simulate inorder traversal without recursion. Push all left children of the current node onto the stack. Pop a node, decrement k; if k reaches 0, that node's value is the answer. Then move to the right child and repeat. O(H+k) time where H is tree height, O(H) space for the stack.
